children.name: Ambystoma
children.description: Stocky burrowing salamanders of North America. The axolotl is permanently neotenic — retaining larval features including spectacular external gills throughout its adult life. Its extraordinary ability to regenerate lost limbs, heart tissue, and portions of the brain makes it one of the most studied animals in regenerative biology. The wild population is critically endangered in a single lake in Mexico City.

children.name: Ambystoma mexicanum
children.description: Neotenic; retains external gills as an adult; can regenerate entire limbs and portions of its heart and brain with perfect fidelity; wild population critically endangered in Lake Xochimilco, Mexico City; millions bred in captivity worldwide.

children.name: Ambystoma tigrinum
children.description: Largest terrestrial salamander in North America at up to 33 cm; some isolated lake populations remain permanently neotenic.

children.name: Ambystoma maculatum
children.description: Photosynthetic algae live inside embryo cells — the only vertebrate with a photosynthetic endosymbiont; adults migrate to breeding ponds on the first warm rainy night of spring.

children.name: Ambystoma opacum
children.description: Breeds in autumn in dry riverbeds, guarding eggs until autumn rains fill the pool; larvae then hatch with a head start over spring-breeding competitors.

children.name: Ambystoma californiense
children.description: Endangered; dependent on California vernal pools; listed under the US Endangered Species Act.
